html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twelve — Part Four

The Book of Shemu'el Aleph

SHEMU'EL ALEPH

The First Book of Samuel

Chapters 25 through 31

Avigayil — Dawid in Exile — Sha'ul's Death — The Book Closes

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QSama (4Q51) — Cave 4

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTERS 25 THROUGH 31 — THE END OF SHA'UL

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 25 Shemu'el Dies — Naval and Avigayil — Avigayil's Wisdom — Dawid Spares Naval

1 Now Shemu'el died. And all Yisra'EL assembled and mourned for him, and they buried him in his house at Ramah. Then Dawid rose and went down to the wilderness of Paran.

2 And there was a man in Ma'on whose business was in Karmel. The man was very rich — he had three thousand sheep and a thousand goats. He was shearing his sheep in Karmel.

3 Now the name of the man was Naval, and the name of his wife Avigayil. The woman was discerning and beautiful, but the man was harsh and badly behaved. He was a Kalevite.

4 Dawid heard in the wilderness that Naval was shearing his sheep.

5 So Dawid sent ten young men. And Dawid said to the young men, Go up to Karmel, and go to Naval and greet him in my name.

6 And thus you shall greet him: Peace be to you, and peace be to your house, and peace be to all that you have.

7 I hear that you have shearers. Now your shepherds have been with us, and we did not harm them, and they missed nothing all the time they were in Karmel.

8 Ask your young men, and they will tell you. Therefore let my young men find favor in your eyes, for we come on a feast day. Please give whatever you have at hand to your servants and to your son Dawid.

9 When Dawid's young men came, they said all this to Naval in the name of Dawid, and then they waited.

10 And Naval answered Dawid's servants, Who is Dawid? Who is the son of Yishai? There are many servants these days who are breaking away from their masters.

11 Shall I take my bread and my water and my meat that I have killed for my shearers and give it to men who come from I do not know where?

12 So Dawid's young men turned away and came back and told him all this.

13 And Dawid said to his men, Every man strap on his sword. And every man of them strapped on his sword. Dawid also strapped on his sword. And about four hundred men went up after Dawid, while two hundred remained with the baggage.

14 But one of the young men told Avigayil, Naval's wife, saying, Behold, Dawid sent messengers out of the wilderness to greet our master, and he railed at them.

15 Yet the men were very good to us, and we suffered no harm, and we did not miss anything when we were in the fields, as long as we went with them.

16 They were a wall to us both by night and by day, all the while we were with them keeping the sheep.

17 Now therefore know this and consider what you should do, for harm is determined against our master and against all his house, and he is such a worthless man that one cannot speak to him.

18 Then Avigayil made haste and took two hundred loaves and two skins of wine and five sheep already prepared and five seahs of parched grain and a hundred clusters of raisins and two hundred cakes of figs, and laid them on donkeys.

19 And she said to her young men, Go on before me — behold, I come after you. But she did not tell her husband Naval.

20 And as she rode on the donkey and came down under cover of the mountain, behold, Dawid and his men came down toward her, and she met them.

21 Now Dawid had said, Surely in vain have I guarded all that this fellow has in the wilderness, so that nothing was missed of all that belonged to him, and he has returned me evil for good.

22 Creator do so to Dawid and more also, if by morning I leave so much as one male of all who belong to him.

23 When Avigayil saw Dawid, she hurried and got down from the donkey and fell before Dawid on her face and bowed to the ground.

24 She fell at his feet and said, On me alone, my lord, be the guilt. Please let your servant speak in your ears, and hear the words of your servant.

25 Let not my lord regard this worthless fellow, Naval, for as his name is, so is he. Naval is his name, and folly is with him. But I your servant did not see the young men of my lord, whom you sent.

26 Now then, my lord, as YHWH lives, and as your soul lives, because YHWH has restrained you from bloodguilt and from saving with your own hand, now then let your enemies and those who seek to do evil to my lord be as Naval.

27 And now let this present that your servant has brought to my lord be given to the young men who follow my lord.

28 Please forgive the trespass of your servant. For YHWH will certainly make my lord a sure house, because my lord is fighting the battles of YHWH, and evil shall not be found in you so long as you live.

29 If men rise up to pursue you and to seek your life, the life of my lord shall be bound in the bundle of the living in the care of YHWH your Creator. And the lives of your enemies he shall sling out as from the hollow of a sling.

30 And when YHWH has done to my lord according to all the good that he has spoken concerning you and has appointed you prince over Yisra'EL,

31 My lord shall have no cause of grief or pangs of conscience for having shed blood without cause or for my lord working salvation himself. And when YHWH has dealt well with my lord, then remember your servant.

32 And Dawid said to Avigayil, Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, who sent you this day to meet me.

33 Blessed be your discretion, and blessed be you, who have kept me this day from bloodguilt and from working salvation with my own hand.

34 For as surely as YHWH the Creator of Yisra'EL lives, who has restrained me from hurting you, unless you had hurried and come to meet me, truly by morning there had not been left to Naval so much as one male.

35 Then Dawid received from her hand what she had brought him. And he said to her, Go up in peace to your house. See, I have obeyed your voice, and I have granted your petition.

36 And Avigayil came to Naval, and behold, he was holding a feast in his house, like the feast of a king. And Naval's heart was merry within him, for he was very drunk. So she told him nothing at all until the morning light.

37 In the morning, when the wine had gone out of Naval, his wife told him these things, and his heart died within him, and he became as a stone.

38 And about ten days later YHWH struck Naval, and he died.

39 When Dawid heard that Naval was dead, he said, Blessed be YHWH who has avenged the insult I received at the hand of Naval, and has kept back his servant from wrongdoing. YHWH has returned the evil of Naval on his own head. Then Dawid sent and spoke to Avigayil, to take her as his wife.

40 When the servants of Dawid came to Avigayil at Karmel, they said to her, Dawid has sent us to you to take you to him as his wife.

41 And she rose and bowed with her face to the ground and said, Behold, your servant is a servant to wash the feet of the servants of my lord.

42 And Avigayil hurried and rose and mounted a donkey, and her five young women attended her. She followed the messengers of Dawid and became his wife.

43 Dawid also took Akhinoam of Yizre'el, and both of them became his wives.

44 Sha'ul had given Mikhal his daughter, Dawid's wife, to Palti the son of Layish, who was of Gallim.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 25. Avigayil's speech — verses 26-31 — is the most sustained piece of covenant theological reasoning delivered by a woman in the Nevi'im. She prevents Dawid from taking the blood vengeance that would have disqualified him from the covenant kingship he is about to inherit.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 25 in full. Verse 29 — the life of my lord shall be bound in the bundle of the living in the care of YHWH — is read as one of the most beautiful covenant declarations of divine protection in the Nevi'im: Dawid's life is held securely in YHWH's keeping, bound in the bundle of the living.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 25 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The death of Shemu'el — verse 1 — is noted in a single verse. The prophet who anointed both Sha'ul and Dawid, who declared the covenant terms of the monarchy, dies without ceremony. The chapter then immediately moves to the Naval narrative.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 32-33 — blessed be YHWH who sent you this day to meet me — blessed be your discretion who kept me from bloodguilt. Dawid recognizes Avigayil as a covenant instrument sent by YHWH. His intended act of vengeance — killing every male of Naval's household — would have been precisely the kind of impulsive violence that disqualifies kings. Avigayil's covenant wisdom intervenes between Dawid's anger and its consequence. Verse 38 — YHWH struck Naval and he died. The judgment Dawid was about to take into his own hands, YHWH executes in his own time. The covenant principle Shemu'el declared — YHWH will judge between us — operates here without Dawid lifting a sword.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 26 Dawid Spares Sha'ul Again — Take the Spear From His Head — YHWH's Anointed

1 Then the Ziphites came to Sha'ul at Giv'ah, saying, Is not Dawid hiding himself on the hill of Khakhilah, which is before Yeshimon?

2 So Sha'ul arose and went down to the wilderness of Ziph with three thousand chosen men of Yisra'EL to seek Dawid in the wilderness of Ziph.

3 And Sha'ul encamped on the hill of Khakhilah, which is beside the road on the east of Yeshimon. But Dawid remained in the wilderness. When he saw that Sha'ul came after him into the wilderness,

4 Dawid sent out spies and learned that Sha'ul had indeed come.

5 Then Dawid arose and came to the place where Sha'ul had encamped. And Dawid saw the place where Sha'ul lay, with Avner the son of Ner, the commander of his army. Sha'ul was lying within the encampment, while the army was encamped around him.

6 Then Dawid said to Akhimelekh the Khittite, and to Avishai the son of Tzeruya, Yoav's brother, Who will go down with me into the camp to Sha'ul? And Avishai said, I will go down with you.

7 So Dawid and Avishai went to the army by night. And there lay Sha'ul sleeping within the encampment, with his spear stuck in the ground at his head, and Avner and the army lay around him.

8 Then Avishai said to Dawid, Creator has given your enemy into your hand this day. Now please let me pin him to the earth with one stroke of the spear, and I will not strike him twice.

9 But Dawid said to Avishai, Do not destroy him, for who can put out his hand against YHWH's anointed and be guiltless?

10 And Dawid said, As YHWH lives, YHWH will strike him, or his day will come to die, or he will go down into battle and perish.

11 YHWH forbid that I should put out my hand against YHWH's anointed. But take now the spear that is at his head and the jar of water, and let us go.

12 So Dawid took the spear and the jar of water from Sha'ul's head, and they went away. No man saw it or knew it, nor did any awake, for they were all asleep, because a deep sleep from YHWH had fallen upon them.

13 Then Dawid went over to the other side and stood far off on the top of the hill, with a great distance between them.

14 And Dawid called to the army, and to Avner the son of Ner, saying, Will you not answer, Avner? Then Avner answered, Who are you who calls to the king?

15 And Dawid said to Avner, Are you not a man? Who is like you in Yisra'EL? Why then have you not kept watch over your lord the king? For one of the people came in to destroy the king your lord.

16 This thing that you have done is not good. As YHWH lives, you deserve to die, because you have not kept watch over your lord, YHWH's anointed. And now see where the king's spear is and the jar of water that was at his head.

17 Sha'ul recognized Dawid's voice and said, Is this your voice, my son Dawid? And Dawid said, It is my voice, my lord, O king.

18 And he said, Why does my lord pursue after his servant? For what have I done? What evil is on my hands?

19 Now therefore let my lord the king hear the words of his servant. If it is YHWH who has stirred you up against me, may he accept an offering, but if it is men, may they be cursed before YHWH, for they have driven me out this day that I should have no share in the heritage of YHWH, saying, Go, serve other gods.

20 Now therefore, let not my blood fall to the earth away from the presence of YHWH, for the king of Yisra'EL has come out to seek a single flea like one who hunts a partridge in the mountains.

21 Then Sha'ul said, I have sinned. Return, my son Dawid, for I will no more do you harm, because my life was precious in your eyes this day. Behold, I have acted foolishly, and have made a great mistake.

22 And Dawid answered and said, Here is the spear, O king. Let one of the young men come over and take it.

23 YHWH rewards every man for his righteousness and his faithfulness, for YHWH gave you into my hand today, and I would not put out my hand against YHWH's anointed.

24 Behold, as your life was precious this day in my sight, so may my life be precious in the sight of YHWH, and may he deliver me out of all tribulation.

25 Then Sha'ul said to Dawid, Blessed be you, my son Dawid. You will do many things and will succeed in them. So Dawid went his way, and Sha'ul returned to his place.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 26. The second sparing of Sha'ul — structurally parallel to Chapter 24 — confirms the covenant principle as Dawid's settled policy rather than a single impulse. YHWH sends a deep sleep on the entire army so that Dawid and Avishai can enter undetected — covenant protection demonstrated at the enemy's own camp.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 26 in full. Verse 23 — YHWH rewards every man for his righteousness and faithfulness — is read as Dawid's covenant theology of action: he acts righteously not to earn reward but because righteousness is the covenant character that YHWH honors.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 26 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The repetition of the sparing motif — twice Dawid has Sha'ul at his mercy, twice he refuses to strike — establishes it as covenant conviction rather than circumstance. The covenant principle does not change with opportunity.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9-10 — who can put out his hand against YHWH's anointed and be guiltless? YHWH will strike him, or his day will come to die, or he will go down into battle and perish. Dawid articulates three possibilities for Sha'ul's end — all of them YHWH's acts rather than Dawid's. The covenant patience Dawid demonstrates is not passivity — it is the active decision to leave judgment to YHWH. Sha'ul's end will come in Chapter 31, in the third way Dawid named — he will fall in battle. Verse 25 — blessed be you, my son Dawid. You will do many things and will succeed in them. Even Sha'ul in his moments of covenant clarity acknowledges what YHWH has ordained. The knowledge does not change his pursuit. He returns to his place and Dawid goes his way.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 27 Dawid Goes to the Pelishtim — Akhish Gives Him Tziklag — Dawid's Raids

1 And Dawid said in his heart, Now I shall perish one day by the hand of Sha'ul. There is nothing better for me than that I should escape to the land of the Pelishtim. Then Sha'ul will despair of seeking me any longer within the borders of Yisra'EL, and I shall escape out of his hand.

2 So Dawid arose and went over, he and the six hundred men who were with him, to Akhish the son of Ma'okh, king of Gat.

3 And Dawid lived with Akhish at Gat, he and his men, every man with his household, and Dawid with his two wives, Akhinoam of Yizre'el, and Avigayil of Karmel, Naval's widow.

4 And when it was told Sha'ul that Dawid had fled to Gat, he no longer sought him.

5 Then Dawid said to Akhish, If I have found favor in your eyes, let a place be given me in one of the country towns, that I may dwell there. For why should your servant dwell in the royal city with you?

6 So that day Akhish gave him Tziklag. Therefore Tziklag has belonged to the kings of Yehudah to this day.

7 And the number of the days that Dawid lived in the country of the Pelishtim was a year and four months.

8 Now Dawid and his men went up and made raids against the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites, for these were the inhabitants of the land from of old, as far as Shur, to the land of Egypt.

9 And Dawid would strike the land and would leave neither man nor woman alive, but would take away the sheep, the oxen, the donkeys, the camels, and the garments, and come back to Akhish.

10 When Akhish asked, Against whom have you made a raid today? Dawid would say, Against the Negev of Yehudah, or Against the Negev of the Yerakhmeel, or Against the Negev of the Kenite.

11 And Dawid would leave neither man nor woman alive to bring news to Gat, thinking, lest they should tell about us and say, So Dawid has done. Such was his custom all the while he lived in the country of the Pelishtim.

12 And Akhish trusted Dawid, thinking, He has made himself an utter stench to his people Yisra'EL. Therefore he shall always be my servant.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 27. The Tziklag period — Dawid living among the Pelishtim — is the most morally ambiguous section of the Dawid narrative. He deceives Akhish while raiding the Amalekites and other enemies of Yisra'EL, leaving no survivors to report the truth.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 27 in full. The covenant tension of Dawid's Tziklag period is read without resolution: the text does not condemn Dawid's deception of Akhish but it does not endorse it either. The narrative preserves the complexity of covenant faithfulness under extreme pressure.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 27 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 1 — Dawid said in his heart, I shall perish one day by the hand of Sha'ul — is one of the few moments in the Dawid narrative where his private assessment is recorded. Even YHWH's anointed, who has been supernaturally protected, experiences the pressure of sustained exile as a crisis of faith.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — I shall perish one day by the hand of Sha'ul. Dawid's assessment in his heart is the covenant contrast with his public declarations of trust. The same man who declared the battle is YHWH's and who twice spared YHWH's anointed, saying YHWH will deal with Sha'ul in his own time — now says in his heart that Sha'ul will kill him. The wilderness years have worn down the covenant confidence. Dawid goes to the Pelishtim not because it is right but because he is afraid. The narrative does not conceal this. The covenant biography of Dawid includes his fear and his failures as fully as his faith and his victories.

SHA'UL'S FINAL DAYS — CHAPTERS 28 THROUGH 31

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 28 Sha'ul and the Medium of En-Dor — Shemu'el's Spirit — Tomorrow You and Your Sons Will Be With Me

1 In those days the Pelishtim gathered their forces for war, to fight against Yisra'EL. And Akhish said to Dawid, Know that you and your men are to go out with me in the army.

2 Dawid said to Akhish, Very well, you shall know what your servant can do. And Akhish said to Dawid, Very well, I will make you my bodyguard for life.

3 Now Shemu'el had died, and all Yisra'EL had mourned for him and buried him in Ramah, his own city. And Sha'ul had put the mediums and the necromancers out of the land.

4 The Pelishtim assembled and came and encamped at Shunem. And Sha'ul gathered all Yisra'EL, and they encamped at Gilboa.

5 When Sha'ul saw the army of the Pelishtim, he was afraid, and his heart trembled greatly.

6 And when Sha'ul inquired of YHWH, YHWH did not answer him, either by dreams, or by Urim, or by prophets.

7 Then Sha'ul said to his servants, Seek out for me a woman who is a medium, that I may go to her and inquire of her. And his servants said to him, Behold, there is a medium at En-Dor.

8 So Sha'ul disguised himself and put on other garments and went, he and two men with him. And they came to the woman by night. And he said, Divine for me by a spirit and bring up for me whomever I shall name to you.

9 The woman said to him, Surely you know what Sha'ul has done, how he has cut off the mediums and the necromancers from the land. Why then are you laying a trap for my life to bring about my death?

10 But Sha'ul swore to her by YHWH, As YHWH lives, no punishment shall come upon you for this thing.

11 Then the woman said, Whom shall I bring up for you? He said, Bring up Shemu'el for me.

12 When the woman saw Shemu'el, she cried out with a loud voice. And the woman said to Sha'ul, Why have you deceived me? You are Sha'ul.

13 The king said to her, Do not be afraid. What do you see? And the woman said to Sha'ul, I see a god coming up out of the earth.

14 He said to her, What is his appearance? And she said, An old man is coming up, and he is wrapped in a robe. And Sha'ul knew that it was Shemu'el, and he bowed with his face to the ground and paid homage.

15 Then Shemu'el said to Sha'ul, Why have you disturbed me by bringing me up? Sha'ul answered, I am in great distress, for the Pelishtim are warring against me, and Creator has turned away from me and answers me no more, either by prophets or by dreams. Therefore I have summoned you to tell me what I shall do.

16 And Shemu'el said, Why then do you ask me, since YHWH has turned from you and become your enemy?

17 YHWH has done to you as he spoke by me, for YHWH has torn the kingdom out of your hand and given it to your neighbor, to Dawid.

18 Because you did not obey the voice of YHWH and did not carry out his fierce wrath against Amalek, therefore YHWH has done this thing to you this day.

19 Moreover, YHWH will give Yisra'EL also with you into the hand of the Pelishtim, and tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me. YHWH will give the army of Yisra'EL also into the hand of the Pelishtim.

20 Then Sha'ul fell at once full length on the ground, filled with fear because of the words of Shemu'el. And there was no strength in him, for he had eaten nothing all day and all night.

21 And the woman came to Sha'ul, and when she saw that he was terrified, she said to him, Behold, your servant has obeyed you. I have taken my life in my hand and have listened to what you have said to me.

22 Now therefore, you also obey your servant. Let me set a morsel of bread before you, and eat, that you may have strength when you go on your way.

23 He refused and said, I will not eat. But his servants, together with the woman, urged him, and he listened to their words. So he arose from the earth and sat on the bed.

24 Now the woman had a fattened calf in the house, and she quickly killed it, and she took flour and kneaded it and baked unleavened bread of it,

25 And she put it before Sha'ul and his servants, and they ate. Then they rose and went away that night.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 28. The En-Dor narrative is one of the most theologically complex passages in the Nevi'im. The Torah forbids consulting the dead — Devarim 18:11. Sha'ul himself expelled the mediums. His desperation drives him to violate the very prohibition he enforced.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 28 in full. Verse 6 — YHWH did not answer him, either by dreams, or by Urim, or by prophets — is read as the covenant silence that results from covenant rejection: Sha'ul expelled the priest with the ephod — Evyatar fled to Dawid — and the prophet Shemu'el is dead. Sha'ul has severed every legitimate covenant channel of communication.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 28 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Whether the figure that appears is truly Shemu'el or a deceptive spirit has been debated in the covenant tradition. The text presents the figure as Shemu'el, and the words spoken are consistent with Shemu'el's covenant declarations in life.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15-19 — Shemu'el's final word to Sha'ul is the covenant sentence delivered from beyond death. Every element is the fulfillment of what was declared in Chapter 15: the kingdom torn from your hand and given to Dawid — because you did not obey the voice of YHWH against Amalek. Tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me. The covenant consequences that Shemu'el declared in life are confirmed from beyond it. Verse 20 — Sha'ul fell at once full length on the ground, filled with fear. The king who was taller than any of the people lies full length on the ground before the word of YHWH delivered through a dead prophet. The covenant word outlasts its speaker.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 29 Dawid Dismissed by the Pelishti Lords — YHWH's Providence in Rejection

1 Now the Pelishtim had gathered all their forces at Afek. And the Yisra'ELites were encamped by the spring that is in Yizre'el.

2 As the lords of the Pelishtim were passing on by hundreds and by thousands, and Dawid and his men were passing on in the rear with Akhish,

3 The commanders of the Pelishtim said, What are these Hebrews doing here? And Akhish said to the commanders of the Pelishtim, Is this not Dawid, the servant of Sha'ul, king of Yisra'EL, who has been with me now for days and years, and since he deserted to me I have found no fault in him to this day?

4 But the commanders of the Pelishtim were angry with him. And the commanders of the Pelishtim said to him, Send the man back, that he may return to the place to which you have assigned him. He shall not go down with us to battle, lest in the battle he become an adversary to us. For how could this fellow reconcile himself to his lord? Would it not be with the heads of the men here?

5 Is not this Dawid, of whom they sing to one another in dances, Sha'ul has struck down his thousands, and Dawid his ten thousands?

6 Then Akhish called Dawid and said to him, As YHWH lives, you have been honest, and to me it seems right that you should march out and in with me in the campaign. For I have found nothing wrong in you from the day of your coming to me to this day. Nevertheless, the lords do not approve of you.

7 So return now and go peaceably, that you may not displease the lords of the Pelishtim.

8 And Dawid said to Akhish, But what have I done? What have you found in your servant from the day I entered your service until now, that I may not go and fight against the enemies of my lord the king?

9 And Akhish answered Dawid, I know that you are as blameless in my sight as an angel of Creator. Nevertheless, the commanders of the Pelishtim have said, He shall not go up with us to the battle.

10 Rise early in the morning with the servants of your lord who came with you, and start early in the morning and depart as soon as you have light.

11 So Dawid set out with his men early in the morning to return to the land of the Pelishtim. But the Pelishtim went up to Yizre'el.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 29. The Pelishti lords' dismissal of Dawid is read as covenant providence: Dawid was being drawn into fighting against Yisra'EL alongside the Pelishtim. YHWH uses the suspicion of the Pelishti commanders to prevent Dawid from a covenant catastrophe.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 29 in full. The women's song that established Dawid above Sha'ul — verse 5 — now protects him by making the Pelishti commanders unwilling to trust him in battle. The same cultural memory that provoked Sha'ul's jealousy now delivers Dawid from fighting against his own people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 29 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Akhish's declaration — as YHWH lives, you have been honest — using YHWH's name is one of the textual details the Nevi'im preserve without theological comment. A Pelishti king swears by YHWH to attest Dawid's character.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — lest in the battle he become an adversary to us. For how could this fellow reconcile himself to his lord? Would it not be with the heads of the men here? The Pelishti commanders ask the right question: when the battle turns, Dawid will fight for Yisra'EL, not for the Pelishtim. Their suspicion is correct and their dismissal is covenant providence. Dawid is spared from the impossible dilemma of fighting against Sha'ul or betraying Akhish. The covenant God who works through Khannah's womb and Yonatan's arrow and Avigayil's wisdom now works through the mistrust of the Pelishti military command.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 30 Tziklag Burned — The Amalekites — Dawid Strengthened Himself in YHWH — All Recovered

1 Now when Dawid and his men came to Tziklag on the third day, the Amalekites had made a raid against the Negev and against Tziklag. They had overcome Tziklag and burned it with fire

2 And taken captive the women and all who were in it, both small and great. They killed no one, but carried them off and went their way.

3 And when Dawid and his men came to the city, they found it burned with fire, and their wives and sons and daughters taken captive.

4 Then Dawid and the people who were with him raised their voices and wept until they had no more strength to weep.

5 Dawid's two wives also had been taken captive, Akhinoam of Yizre'el and Avigayil the widow of Naval of Karmel.

6 And Dawid was greatly distressed, for the people spoke of stoning him, because all the people were bitter in soul, each for his sons and daughters. But Dawid strengthened himself in YHWH his Creator.

7 And Dawid said to Evyatar the priest, the son of Akhimelekh, Please bring me the ephod. So Evyatar brought the ephod to Dawid.

8 And Dawid inquired of YHWH, Shall I pursue after this band? Shall I overtake them? He answered him, Pursue, for you shall surely overtake and shall surely rescue.

9 So Dawid set out, and the six hundred men who were with him, and they came to the brook Besor, where those who were left behind stayed.

10 But Dawid pursued, he and four hundred men. Two hundred stayed behind, who were too exhausted to cross the brook Besor.

11 They found an Egyptian in the open country and brought him to Dawid. And they gave him bread and he ate. They gave him water to drink,

12 And they gave him a piece of a cake of figs and two clusters of raisins. And when he had eaten, his spirit revived, for he had not eaten bread or drunk water for three days and three nights.

13 And Dawid said to him, To whom do you belong? And where are you from? He said, I am a young man of Egypt, servant to an Amalekite, and my master left me behind because I fell sick three days ago.

14 We had made a raid against the Negev of the Kerethites and against that which belongs to Yehudah and against the Negev of Kalev, and we burned Tziklag with fire.

15 And Dawid said to him, Will you take me down to this band? And he said, Swear to me by Creator that you will not kill me or deliver me into the hands of my master, and I will take you down to this band.

16 And when he had taken him down, behold, they were spread abroad over all the land, eating and drinking and dancing, because of all the great spoil they had taken from the land of the Pelishtim and from the land of Yehudah.

17 And Dawid struck them from twilight until the evening of the next day, and not a man of them escaped, except four hundred young men, who mounted camels and fled.

18 Dawid recovered all that the Amalekites had taken, and Dawid rescued his two wives.

19 Nothing was missing, whether small or great, sons or daughters, spoil or anything that had been taken. Dawid brought back all.

20 Dawid also captured all the flocks and herds, and the people drove the livestock before him, and said, This is Dawid's spoil.

21 Then Dawid came to the two hundred men who had been too exhausted to follow Dawid, and who had been left at the brook Besor. And they went out to meet Dawid and to meet the people who were with him. And when Dawid came near to the people he greeted them.

22 Then all the wicked and worthless fellows among the men who had gone with Dawid said, Because they did not go with us, we will not give them any of the spoil that we have recovered, except that each man may lead away his wife and children, and depart.

23 But Dawid said, You shall not do so, my brothers, with what YHWH has given us. He has preserved us and given into our hand the band that came against us.

24 Who would listen to you in this matter? For as his share is who goes down into the battle, so shall his share be who stays by the baggage. They shall share alike.

25 And he made it a statute and a rule for Yisra'EL from that day forward to this day.

26 When Dawid came to Tziklag, he sent part of the spoil to his friends, the elders of Yehudah, saying, Here is a present for you from the spoil of the enemies of YHWH.

27 It was for those in Beit-El, in Ramot of the Negev, in Yattir,

28 In Aro'er, in Sifmot, in Eshtemoa,

29 In Rakhel, in the cities of the Yerakhmeel, in the cities of the Kenite,

30 In Khormah, in Bor-Ashan, in Ataakh,

31 In Khevron, for all the places where Dawid and his men had roamed.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 30. Verse 6 — Dawid strengthened himself in YHWH his Creator — is one of the most significant covenant actions in Shemu'el Aleph. At the lowest point of his exile — his city burned, his wives taken, his men threatening to stone him — Dawid does not despair or react with violence but strengthens himself in YHWH.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 30 in full. The covenant ruling of verse 24 — as his share is who goes down into the battle, so shall his share be who stays by the baggage — is read as Dawid's first covenant legislative act: establishing the principle of equal distribution between combatants and support personnel, which becomes a statute in Yisra'EL.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 30 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The Egyptian servant abandoned by the Amalekites — verse 11-15 — is another example of the covenant pattern: the marginalized and abandoned person becomes the instrument through which YHWH accomplishes the covenant purpose.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — Dawid strengthened himself in YHWH his Creator. The Hebrew vayitkhazek — and he strengthened himself — is the covenant act of interior spiritual fortification at the moment of maximum external pressure. His city is ashes. His wives are captives. His men are threatening his life. He inquires of YHWH before acting. He pursues on YHWH's word. He recovers everything. The covenant sequence is precise: crisis — strengthening in YHWH — inquiry — obedience — complete recovery. Verse 19 — nothing was missing, whether small or great, sons or daughters, spoil or anything. The covenant completeness of the recovery mirrors the covenant completeness of the loss. YHWH restores what the enemy took in full.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 31 The Death of Sha'ul — Yonatan Falls — Yisra'EL Flees — The Men of Yavesh-Gil'ad Honor Sha'ul

1 Now the Pelishtim fought against Yisra'EL, and the men of Yisra'EL fled before the Pelishtim and fell slain on Mount Gilboa.

2 And the Pelishtim overtook Sha'ul and his sons, and the Pelishtim struck down Yonatan and Avinadav and Malkishua, the sons of Sha'ul.

3 The battle pressed hard against Sha'ul, and the archers found him, and he was badly wounded by the archers.

4 Then Sha'ul said to his armor-bearer, Draw your sword, and thrust me through with it, lest these uncircumcised come and thrust me through, and mistreat me. But his armor-bearer would not, for he feared greatly. Therefore Sha'ul took his own sword and fell upon it.

5 And when his armor-bearer saw that Sha'ul was dead, he also fell upon his sword and died with him.

6 Thus Sha'ul died, and his three sons, and his armor-bearer, and all his men, on the same day together.

7 And when the men of Yisra'EL who were on the other side of the valley and those beyond the Yarden saw that the men of Yisra'EL had fled and that Sha'ul and his sons were dead, they abandoned their cities and fled. And the Pelishtim came and lived in them.

8 The next day, when the Pelishtim came to strip the slain, they found Sha'ul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa.

9 So they cut off his head and stripped off his armor and sent messengers throughout the land of the Pelishtim, to carry the good news to the house of their idols and to the people.

10 They put his armor in the temple of the Ashtarots, and they fastened his body to the wall of Beit-She'an.

11 But when the inhabitants of Yavesh-Gil'ad heard what the Pelishtim had done to Sha'ul,

12 All the valiant men arose and went all night and took the body of Sha'ul and the bodies of his sons from the wall of Beit-She'an, and they came to Yavesh and burned them there.

13 And they took their bones and buried them under the tamarisk tree in Yavesh and fasted seven days.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 31. The death of Sha'ul closes Shemu'el Aleph. Sha'ul dies on the same battlefield where the men of Yavesh-Gil'ad had been rescued by his first covenant act of kingship in Chapter 11. The men of Yavesh-Gil'ad honor him with the last act of the book.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 31 in full. The death of Yonatan — verse 2 — is recorded in a single clause. The greatest covenant friend in Shemu'el Aleph falls in the same battle as the father he served and loved and disagreed with. The men of Yavesh-Gil'ad who bury Sha'ul are the same people he saved when the Spirit first rushed upon him.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 31 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Sha'ul's death — falling on his own sword — fulfills the word Shemu'el spoke at En-Dor: tomorrow you and your sons shall be with me. The third of the three possibilities Dawid named in Chapter 26:10 — he will go down into battle and perish — is the one that comes to pass.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-13 — the men of Yavesh-Gil'ad arose and went all night and took the body of Sha'ul from the wall of Beit-She'an. The men whose right eyes Nakhash threatened to gouge out — who sent the desperate message that became the occasion of Sha'ul's first great covenant act — honor him with the last act of the book. Covenant memory is not short. They travel through the night to retrieve the body of the man who came to their rescue forty years before. Shemu'el Aleph ends with a burial and a fast. The anointed king is dead. The wilderness fugitive will become king. The covenant gap between promise and fulfillment closes in Shemu'el Bet.

END OF PART FOUR — CHAPTERS 25 THROUGH 31 — SHEMU'EL ALEPH COMPLETE
